topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The reception hall buzzed with the kind of loud, happy chaos that always seemed to amplify awkwardness. I was trying to navigate the crowded dance floor, pretending to be deeply engaged in a conversation with a distant acquaintance, when a familiar silhouette caught my eye near the buffet table. It was him. Liam. He was standing next to Sarah, beaming, looking every bit the picture of a successful, happy man. My entire internal system seemed to seize up. My palms instantly slicked with sweat, and I felt a sudden, desperate urge to melt into the nearest potted palm. I managed a strangled, "Oh, hey," the words scraping out of my throat like gravel. Sarah turned, and the air seemed to thin around me. The smile I offered felt brittle, stretched thin over a rapidly collapsing internal structure. I quickly pivoted, executing a clumsy maneuver that involved bumping into a passing waiter, and fled toward the relative sanctuary of the balcony overlooking the garden, needing desperately to breathe air that didn't smell like expensive champagne and lingering regret.

[story 2]

I had rehearsed a hundred scenarios for the evening, but none prepared me for the sudden, jarring appearance of Alex. He materialized from the throng of guests, and for a full three seconds, the world narrowed down to just the space between us. My breath hitched, a sharp, painful intake that I desperately tried to disguise as a normal gasp. My gaze locked onto his, and I felt a distinct, hot flush creep up my neck, staining my cheeks a deep crimson. Every muscle in my jaw tightened, and I focused intensely on the pattern of the marble floor beneath my shoes, willing myself to become invisible. When he finally approached, offering a friendly wave, my response was a jerky nod, my voice coming out surprisingly high and thin. "Hi," I managed, the sound feeling foreign and inadequate. I could feel the weight of every eye in the room, and I spent the next few minutes performing a frantic, unnecessary series of movements—adjusting my dress, fiddling with my drink—anything to avoid making sustained eye contact with the one person I was actively trying to erase from my immediate perception.

[story 3]

The music was pumping, the lighting was warm, and I was attempting to look casual while nursing a lukewarm cocktail. Then, there he was. Mark. He was laughing, his head tilted back, and the sight of him—so vibrant, so utterly present—hit me like a physical blow. A cold dread washed over my stomach, making my limbs feel heavy and unresponsive. I watched him for a beat too long, cataloging every detail, before a wave of sheer panic seized control. I immediately started scanning the room, searching for an escape route, a wall, anything to block his view. When our paths inevitably crossed near the cake table, I felt a profound need to disappear. I offered a strained smile that didn't reach my eyes, a tight, almost painful expression. "Good to see you," I mumbled, the words tasting like ash. I practically bolted toward the edge of the room, needing the vast, empty space outside the immediate vicinity of his presence to regain some semblance of control over my rapidly fraying composure.
